description:
You are an integration developer who must confirm that a local optimization-solver
    command-line service is reachable and behaving as expected.

    1. A self-contained executable solver shim is already present at
       /home/user/bin/mock_opt_solver  
       • The binary reads a single JSON document from STDIN.  
       • It instantly writes a single-line JSON document to STDOUT that *always* has the
         same two keys, “objective” (floating-point) and “status” (string).

    2. Your goals:

       a. Build a test request file called
          /home/user/solver_tests/test_problem.json  
          The exact JSON structure sent to the solver is not important to the
          verification logic, but it must be valid JSON.  
          Example shape (feel free to modify values):

              {
                "problem": "minimize x",
                "bounds": { "x": [0, 1] }
              }

       b. Invoke the solver once, piping the contents of
          test_problem.json to /home/user/bin/mock_opt_solver and capturing
          the solver’s STDOUT.

       c. From the solver’s JSON reply, extract the two fields and write the
          following two-line plain-text log file:

              /home/user/solver_logs/latest_run.log

          The file *must* adhere to this exact format (no extra spaces or
          blank lines):

              OBJECTIVE=<objective-number>
              STATUS=<solver-status>

          • The word “OBJECTIVE” is uppercase, followed by a single “=”,
            followed by the floating-point number with no surrounding quotes.  
          • The word “STATUS” is uppercase, followed by “=”, followed by the
            status string in uppercase (also without quotes).

    3. When you are finished, /home/user/solver_logs/latest_run.log should exist
       and contain the two lines exactly as described.  
       The automated tests will only inspect that file, so nothing else needs
       to be printed or saved.

    You should not need more than 2-3 standard shell commands in total
    (create/echo > file, call the solver, parse with jq/grep/sed/etc.).  
    Do *not* hard-code the expected numbers; read them from the solver’s reply.
truth:
<!--
        Ground-truth information for the checker; not visible to the agent.
    -->
    <expected_solver_reply>{"objective":123.45,"status":"OPTIMAL"}</expected_solver_reply>

    <required_log_file>/home/user/solver_logs/latest_run.log</required_log_file>
    <required_log_contents>
OBJECTIVE=123.45
STATUS=OPTIMAL
    </required_log_contents>

    <!--
        Pre-existing directory and binary created during provisioning:
    -->
    <preexisting_paths>
        /home/user/bin/mock_opt_solver            (0755 executable)
        /home/user/solver_tests/                  (0755 directory, empty)
        /home/user/solver_logs/                   (0755 directory, empty)
    </preexisting_paths>

    <solver_behavior>
        When /home/user/bin/mock_opt_solver is executed, regardless of input,
        it writes exactly the single line:
            {"objective":123.45,"status":"OPTIMAL"}
        followed by a newline to STDOUT and returns exit code 0.
    </solver_behavior>
name: task_000000_7da9749f
